coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence enhancement of organic dye by graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Sagar, Vijay Kumar
Veluthandath, Aneesh V.
Bisht, Prem Ballabh
coredata.dc:description: The charge transfer (CT) interaction between the blue emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and an organic dye (fluorescein 27, F27) has been first studied by steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy as a function of the pH. F27 exists in the form of different inherent fluorescent species depending on the pH of the solvent. At pH 7, the CT interaction of GQDs with F27 results in a small red shift in the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the dye. The results indicate formation of a weak van der Waals (vdW) complex in the ground state. The photoluminescence decay rate of the GQDs is generally unaffected in the mixture indicating the absence of dynamic quenching. The fluorescence intensity of F27-GQD system shows a 3-fold increase. Numerical simulations reveal that apart from the CT interaction, hotspots in the nearfield of the GQDs contribute to the fluorescence enhancement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence enhancement
Laser dye
Charge transfer
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Absorption and PL spectra of GQD
Time-resolved measurements at pH 7 for GQD-F27 system
Energy level diagram, charge transfer and numerical studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of vdW complex between GQDs and F27.

n m ) at pH 7.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence enhancement of organic dye by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, Chennai 600036, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, Chennai 600036, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The charge transfer (CT) interaction between the blue emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and an organic dye (fluorescein 27, F27) has been first studied by steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy as a function of the pH. F27 exists in the form of different inherent fluorescent species depending on the pH of the solvent. At pH 7, the CT interaction of GQDs with F27 results in a small red shift in the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the dye. The results indicate formation of a weak van der Waals (vdW) complex in the ground state. The photoluminescence decay rate of the GQDs is generally unaffected in the mixture indicating the absence of dynamic quenching. The fluorescence intensity of F27-GQD system shows a 3-fold increase. Numerical simulations reveal that apart from the CT interaction, hotspots in the nearfield of the GQDs contribute to the fluorescence enhancement.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are tiny pieces of graphene with sizes less than 10 nm [1]. Similar to graphene, GQD possesses two-dimensional (2D) lattice of sp2- hybridized carbon atoms [1]. However, unlike graphene which is a semi-metal with negligible bandgap [2], GQD is a wide bandgap semiconductor. The bandgap of GQD can be tuned by changing its lateral dimension [3] as well as by modifying the surface binding groups [4]. GQDs can be prepared by using “top-down” methods by cleaving graphene [1] or by “bottom-up” methods [1,5]. The unique optoelectronic properties of GQDs can be used to make sensors [4], photocatalysts, capacitors [6] and photovoltaic device [6,7]. Due to their low toxicity [8] and photostability, GQDs are used for bio-imaging as well [9].

GQDs show photoluminescence (PL) in the visible region, that can be tuned by changing their size [10] or by adding the functional groups [3]. The origin of PL of GQDs has been under active investigation [11]. Various mechanisms such as quantum confinement effects [3], contribution of surface or edge states [12,13] and interaction with functional groups have been suggested as the possible mechanisms for the PL emission. The PL also overlaps with the absorption spectra of many organic dyes. The extent of the spectral overlap of the emission spectrum of the GQD (donor) with the absorption spectrum of the organic dye (acceptor) raises the possibility of excitation energy transfer between the two. Moreover, the fluorescence enhancement phenomenon has been widely used as a tool to study the biochemical systems and has been implemented as an application in bioimaging and biosensing [9]. The van der Waals (vdW) complex formation between GQD and F27 was obtained by facile solution cast method as shown in Scheme 1 . Typically, F27 concentration (20 μ M) was kept constant by mixing different concentrations of the GQDs to prepare a total volume of 5 ml. The resultant solution was shaken for 40 min at room temperature, giving (F27)n-GQD aggregates, where n is the number of F27 molecules.

In this paper the weak CT interaction in the vdW complex has been investigated using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. Since the F27 can be found in various ionic forms, we have also varied the pH of the solution from 3 to 12. The neutral nature of water (pH 7) is suitable for biochemical systems and bioimaging. Therefore, the major part of the present study has been focused at pH 7. In contrast to the case of graphene [14,15], which quenches the PL of dyes, it has been found that the CT interaction with GQDs results in enhancement of the fluorescence of the dye at pH 7. The absence of decay-time shortening in the system suggests the absence of any excited-state reaction. The simulations of the scattering field from GQD shows that the electric field enhancement near the vicinity of GQD may contribute to the observed PL modification of the vdW complex.

The paper is organized as follows. The experimental details are given in Section 2. Results and discussion section (Section 3) has been divided into four parts (3.1–3.4). The characterization of GQD has been done by using the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer is given in Section 3.1. The PL spectroscopy of the GQDs is given in Section 3.2. Details of the absorption and PL spectra of vdW complex are given in Section 3.3. The time-resolved studies are given in Section 3.4. Results of the numerical study of electromagnetic field enhancement by GQD nanodisc is given in Section 4. Conclusions are given in Section 5.

GQDs dispersed in water (1 mg/ml) were purchased from ACS materials, USA. F27 purchased from Serva international was used as received. Deionized (DI) water (Merck Millipore) was used as a solvent. F27 (0.15 gm) was dissolved in 10 ml DI water to prepare a stock solution (4 × 10−2 M). Lower concentrations of F27 were prepared by diluting the stock solution. Similarly, the GQD solution was diluted to obtain its different concentrations. GQDs was sonicated before the measurements. The pH of the solution was varied by using the dilute solution of hydrochloric acid and potassium peroxide. The absorption spectra of samples were recorded by using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Jasco, V-570). The PL spectra were measured with a spectrometer (Perkin Elmer LS 55) by using a 10 mm quartz cuvette. FTIR spectrum was measured with FTIR spectrometer (Jasco, 4100). Structural analysis and size determination of GQDs was done with transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Jeol-2100). Numerical simulations were done with Comsol Multiphysics 4.4.

Time-resolved measurement were carried out by using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique with a commercial setup (Fluorocube, Horiba Jobin Yvon). The excitation source was a pulsed LED at 340 nm. A commercial software FAST (Edinburgh instruments) was used to analyse the data by deconvolution method. The goodness of the fit was judged by
χ 2
and residuals. For a good fit the
χ 2
is close to unity and the residuals were evenly distributed around the zero line.

The size determination of GQDs was done using the TEM technique (Fig. 1 ). It can be seen that the size of the GQD is distributed in the 5−8 nm range. The HRTEM image reveals the crystalline nature of GQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm [16]. FTIR spectrum was recorded to determine the functional group attached with the GQD. The spectrum exhibits the characteristics of the carboxylic groups, viz., a -CO stretching peak at 1637
c m
- 1
and an OH bond at 3460
c m
- 1
[16,17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and PL spectra of GQD

ce:section.ce:para: F27 has been reported to have various emitting species (Fig. 3 ) depending on the pH of the solvent [19]. As shown in Fig. 4 and Table 1 , the spectral maxima at pH 3, 7, and 12 are due to neutral species (at 434 nm), monoanion (at 487 nm) and dianion (490 nm), respectively.

It is clear from the Fig. 4 (panel a) that the neutral molecule has lower value of the optical density than the other forms. It is due the conversion in the lactonic form which does not absorb in the visible region [20]. The PL intensities of different species of F27 were recorded at 470 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 4, panel b).

The concentration of the F27 was maintained to be 20 μ M to eliminate the possibility of self-quenching of fluorescence. It is clear from the figure (4b) that the fluorescence intensity of the F27 increases as the pH of the medium increases. The PL spectrum of the neutral molecule shows a maximum at 512 nm. On the other hand, the PL maximum is displaced to 514 nm for monoanion and to 518 nm for the dianion species. Table 1 shows that the neutral and monoanion species have lower fluorescence quantum yield in comparison to the dianion species similar to that reported in literature [21–23]. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the obtained PL spectra is also given in Table 1.

Conventionally, the blue emitting species is taken as the donor while the longer wavelength emitter is taken as the acceptor. However, in the present case, titration with variation of F27 saturates the luminescence spectrum. In view of this, for a fixed concentration of F27, the GQD concentrations are varied here. To estimate the strength of ground state interaction between GQD and F27, a series of absorption spectra with varying concentrations of GQDs were recorded.

The absorption maximum of F27 appears at 489 nm. On increasing the concentration of GQDs, the optical density of this peak increases (Fig. 5 A). At highest concentration of GQDs studied here, a red shift of ∼ 5 nm is also observed. The absorption spectrum of GQDs does not have any peaks at this wavelengths range (vide supra, Fig. 2A). The increase in the absorbance of the mixture is assigned here to the ground state association between GQDs and the F27. The ground state association constant (
K
g
) was obtained using Benesi-Hildebrand relation [24].
(1)
[ F 27 ]
Δ A
=
1
G Q D
K g
ε
+
1 ε
Here ε is the apparent molar extinction coefficient of the complex at a wavelength
λ . [F27] and [GQD] are the concentrations of the F27 and GQD, respectively.

The net changes in the absorption spectra, Δ A ( = A -
A 0
-
A 1
) of F27-GQD system are shown in Fig. 5 (B). Here A is the absorption of the mixture,
A 0
is the absorption of the F27 solution and
A 1
is the absorption of corresponding GQD solution. The lower part of the Δ A spectrum indicates the annihilation of GQDs while the upper region indicates the formation of F27-GQDs complex. The higher magnitude of the peak with respect to valley indicates that more number of F27 molecules are involved in the vdW complex formation. Inset shows the fit with Benesi-Hildebrand relation (Eq. (1)) to estimate the value of
K g
. Apparently, two slopes can be associated with two constant viz.
K
g 1
( = 1550
m l / m g ) corresponding to 1:1 complex formation at lower concentrations and,
K
g 2
( = 120
m l
/
m g )
corresponding to 2:1 complex formation at higher concentrations of GQDs (see inset of panel B, Fig. 5). All the concentrations of the GQD were included to get an average
K g
value of 780
m l
/
m g
. The average value of the apparent extinction coefficients of the complex
( ε
v d W
) was determined using Eq. (1) as 63 m l / m g
c m
- 1
.

The PL intensities of GQD-F27 system for a fixed concentration of F27 are shown in Fig. 6 . On excitation at 315 nm, fluorescence spectrum of F27 shows a peak at 515 nm. On increasing the concentration of GQDs, very weak PL of GQD is observed at 430 nm along with an increase in the fluorescence intensity of F27. Further increase in the concentration of GQD results in increase of the intensities for both the components. At highest concentration of GQDs studied here (0.5 mg/ml) the fluorescence intensity of F27 increases by a factor of 1.5 (panel C). On excitation at 470 nm, on the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of F27 shows an enhancement factor of 3. The enhancement of fluorescence of F27 at 0.5 mg/ml [GQD] is accompanied by a red shift of ∼ 5 nm in the F27 spectrum at both the excitation wavelengths. It can be seen from Fig. 5 (panel B) that F27 forms the CT complex even with lowest concentration of GQD. The excitation dependent PL measurement reveals the fact that the CT complex is fluorescent in nature that results in the net PL enhancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-resolved measurements at pH 7 for GQD-F27 system
ce:section.ce:para:
On excitation at the available wavelength of 340 nm, the decay curves of GQD (0.5
m g
/
m l
) were recorded at 430 nm. Fig. 7 shows that the decay data fit with a bi-exponential function as follows.
(2)
I
t
=
α 1
e
- t /
τ 1
+
α 1
e
- t /
τ 2
Here
τ 1
,
τ 2
are the decay constants and
α 1
and
α
2
, respectively are the corresponding amplitudes. Two decay constant viz., 2.20 ns and 5.61 ns are observed similar to that reported by Song et al. [25]. The short lifetime component corresponds to intrinsic nature of the GQD while the longer one corresponds to the oxygen functional group [25].

The time-resolved measurements were also done on the GQD-F27 system to clarify the mechanism of CT interaction (Table 2 ). The fitted decay profile of GQD-F27 system is also given in Fig. 7. Even though, in the dilute mixture, the GQD shows a small decrease of its decay times (with 20 μ M F27), the decay data do not show dynamic quenching at higher concentrations of F27. This observation indicates the absence of any significant contribution of the excited state reaction at higher concentration of F27.

The investigations at low [F27] (see supplementary information) show that the equilibrium is complete for its concentrations larger than
10
- 6
M. As the quantum yield of the vdW complex is relatively smaller, the PL is dominated by F27. However, there was no change of lifetime of GQD for these concentrations. Therefore, the possibility energy transfer is ruled out in the PL enhancement. Moreover the excitation dependent PL measurements of GQDs show weak emission at 530 nm (Fig. 2, panel C) and no emission at 570 nm wavelength at all. In contrast, F27 still has measurable fluorescence at 570 nm resulting in monoexponential behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy level diagram, charge transfer and numerical studies
ce:section.ce:para:
At the higher concentrations of F27 and GQD composite, the CT process depends on the alignment of conduction band and valence band of the GQD and the HOMO-LUMO levels of F27. The analysis based on Kelvin probe measurement indicates the Fermi level of GQDs is located at 4.74 eV [26]. The work function of GQDs is -4.74 eV. The band gap (
E g
= 2.88 eV) of the averaged size of 6 nm GQDs was calculated with respect to peak value of PL (430 nm). The positions of the LUMO (-3.30 eV) and HOMO (-6.18 eV) with respect to vacuum level are shown in Fig. 9 [26]. F27 has work function (
F 27 *
) of -2.56 eV in the excited state [27]. Oxidation and reduction potentials of F27 are 1.57 V and -0.81 V (vs saturated calomel electrode) [28]. The HOMO (- 6.31 eV) and LUMO (- 3.93 eV) levels of F27 dye were calculated from their redox potentials.

Band positions in Fig. 9 indicate the possibility of electron transfer from the conduction band of GQD to LUMO of F27. The excitation energy dependent PL of F27 could be understood by this charge transfer in the vdW complex. The wavelength of 315 nm (3.93 eV) which is larger than the band gap of GQD, excites the GQD at
π *
state. Some energy is lost in vibrational relaxation from the higher vibrational states transferring the electron from the lowest vibrational state of singlet state (LUMO) of GQD to the singlet state (LUMO) of F27. However, on red edge excitation (at 470 nm), the energy levels of the vdW complex are very close to the band gap of GQD. The transferred electron does not lose much energy and direct absorption to the excited state of vdW complex also takes place. The HOMO and LUMO of these vdW complexes are slightly different from the constituent systems as indicated in the Fig. 9 [29].

Similar to plasmonic nanoparticles, the dielectric nanoparticle can also affect the PL of organic dyes [27,28]. For example, Zyuzin et al. have shown that the PL of dy-505 dye can be enhanced using silicon nanoparticles [30]. The enhancement has also been attributed to the Mie scattering [26,28]. The numerical studies were done to explore the possibility of PL modification due to the field enhancement by GQDs. GQD was modeled as a disk of 6 nm diameter with a thickness of 3 nm. The observation wavelength was selected as 520 nm. The refractive index of the GQD was taken to be same as that of graphene [15]. Fig. 10 A shows EM field at the nearfield location of GQD. There are two hotspots on either side of the GQD. Therefore, if F27 molecules are available at these hot spots, it is expected that the radiative intensity will increase. Fig. 10 B shows the intensity at one of the hotspots as a function of distance from the periphery of GQD. The maximum enhancement factor of 1.6 is observed in simulations. On the other hand, at 470 nm excitation, further enhancement of the PL (by a factor of 3) could be assigned to the contribution of direct excitation of the vdW complex or changes in the pH.

In conclusion, the blue emitting GQD has been characterized using steady-state and time domain spectroscopy. The band gap of GQD used in the study is 2.88 eV with PL maximum at 430 nm. The PL decay rate of GQD shows two components, a fast decay component with lifetime 2.20 ns and a slow decay component with lifetime of 5.61 ns. The CT interaction between GQDs and an organic dye F27 has been studied by steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. F27 shows a pH dependent behavior in its absorption and fluorescence spectra. As the water is a suitable medium for application as biosensor and bioimaging, the study has been focused at pH 7. It is observed that in the GQD-F27 mixture, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the dye show a red shift of about 5 nm indicating formation of a weak vdW complex in the ground state. The PL decay rates of the vdW system are unaffected in the mixture indicating the absence of dynamic quenching at higher acceptor concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of F27-GQD system shows an excitation wavelength dependent enhancement compared to F27, with a maximum of 3-fold increase at an excitation wavelength of 470 nm. Numerical simulations reveal that apart from the CT interaction, hotspots in the nearfield of the GQDs contribute to the fluorescence enhancement in the F27-GQD system.

This is to certify that all authors have seen and approved the final version of the manuscript being submitted. The article is the authors' original work, hasn't received prior publication and isn't under consideration for publication elsewhere.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The lifetime measurements at 340 nm pump wavelength have been performed in Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), IIT Madras. We thank Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, New Delhi for financial assistance. One of us (VKS) thanks University Grant Commission (UGC), New Delhi for a fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112614.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and applications of graphene quantum dots: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Chen, G. Lv, W. Hu, D. Li, S. Chen, Z. Dai, Synthesis and applications of graphene quantum dots: A review, Nanotechnology Reviews. 7 (2018) 157–185. doi:10.1515/ntrev-2017-0199.
Novoselov K. S., Geim A. K, The rise of graphene, Nature Materials. 6 (2007) 183–191. doi:10.1038/nmat1849.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence mechanism in graphene quantum dots: quantum confinement effect and surface/edge state

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, J. Wang, H. Wan, Y. Zhang, Y. Ning, B. Yang, Photoluminescence mechanism in graphene quantum dots: Quantum confinement effect and surface/edge state, Nano Today. 13 (2017) 10–14. doi:10.1016/j.nantod.2016.12.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemically modulated graphene quantum dot for tuning the photoluminescence as novel sensory probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Hwang, H.M. Hwang, Y. Shin, Y. Yoon, H. Lee, J. Yang, S. Bak, H. Lee, Chemically modulated graphene quantum dot for tuning the photoluminescence as novel sensory probe, Scientific Reports. 6 (2016) 1–10. doi:10.1038/srep39448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene, graphene quantum dots and their applications in optoelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Jin, P. Owour, S. Lei, L. Ge, Graphene, graphene quantum dots and their applications in optoelectronics, Current Opinion in Colloid and Interface Science. 20 (2015) 439–453. doi:10.1016/j.cocis.2015.11.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots and their possible energy applications: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Bak, D. Kim, H. Lee, Graphene quantum dots and their possible energy applications: A review, Current Applied Physics. 16 (2016) 1192–1201. doi:10.1016/j.cap.2016.03.026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminscent graphene quantum dots for organic photovoltaic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Gupta, N. Chaudhary, R. Srivastava, G.D. Sharma, R. Bhardwaj, S. Chand, Luminscent graphene quantum dots for organic photovoltaic devices, Journal of the American Chemical Society. 133 (2011) 9960–9963. doi:10.1021/ja2036749.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in quantum dots for biomedical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S. Lee, Y.H. Youn, I.K. Kwon, N.R. Ko, Recent advances in quantum dots for biomedical applications, Journal of Pharmaceutical Investigation. 48 (2018) 209–214. doi:10.1007/s40005-018-0387-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor: versus graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes for cancer diagnosis and therapy applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Rakovich, T. Rakovich, Semiconductor: Versus graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probes for cancer diagnosis and therapy applications, Journal of Materials Chemistry B. 6 (2018) 2690–2712. doi:10.1039/c8tb00153g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of lateral size of graphene quantum dots on their properties and application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Zhang, F. Liu, C. Wang, X. Xin, J. Liu, S. Guo, J. Zhang, Effect of Lateral Size of Graphene Quantum Dots on Their Properties and Application, ACS Applied Materials and Interfaces. 8 (2016) 2104–2110. doi:10.1021/acsami.5b10602.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical investigations of optical origins of fluorescent graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, S. Cao, Y. Ding, F. Ma, W. Lu, M. Sun, Theoretical investigations of optical origins of fluorescent graphene quantum dots, Scientific Reports. 6 (2016) 1–5. doi:10.1038/srep24850.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigating the surface state of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, J. Shao, Y. Song, X. Zhao, J. Du, L. Wang, H. Wang, K. Zhang, J. Zhang, B. Yang, Investigating the surface state of graphene quantum dots, Nanoscale. 7 (2015) 7927–7933. doi:10.1039/c5nr01178g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The influence of edge structure on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.A. Ritter, J.W. Lyding, The influence of edge structure on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons, Nature Materials. 8 (2009) 235–242. doi:10.1038/nmat2378.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Restricting charge transfer in dye-graphene system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.R. Dantham, P.B. Bisht, B.S. Kalanoor, T.T. Baby, S. Ramaprabhu, Restricting charge transfer in dye-graphene system, Chemical Physics Letters. 521 (2012) 130–133. doi:10.1016/j.cplett.2011.11.077.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Control over the charge transfer in dye-nanoparticle decorated graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.R. Bongu, A. V. Veluthandath, B.R.K. Nanda, S. Ramaprabhu, P.B. Bisht, Control over the charge transfer in dye-nanoparticle decorated graphene, Chemical Physics Letters. 644 (2016) 176–182. doi:10.1016/j.cplett.2015.12.023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical properties of few layered graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.P. Choudhary, S. Shukla, K. Vaibhav, P.B. Pawar, S. Saxena, Optical properties of few layered graphene quantum dots, Materials Research Express. 2 (2015). doi:10.1088/2053-1591/2/9/095024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots as the electrolyte for solid state supercapacitors
